coredata.dc:title: One-step synthesis of CDs/BiOBr composite with high exposure of (001) facet: The effect of triisopropanolamine as the carbon source
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Lin
Fan, Xuexue
Cao, Yongqian
Liu, Jianxin
Fan, Caimei
Wang, Yawen
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) modified BiOBr composites were synthesized by a facile one-step hydrothermal method using triisopropanolamine (TIPA). TIPA acts not only as the carbon source of CDs, but also as a structure modifier to obtain mesoporous BiOBr with dominant exposed (001) facets in the composite. Moreover, the amounts of CDs loaded are determined by the effect of TIPA on the pH of the reaction system rather than the addition amount itself. CDs/BiOBr exhibit enhanced adsorption and photodegradation activity of methyl orange compared with pure BiOBr. This work provides a facile approach in the structure controlling synthesis strategy of CDs-based nanohybrids.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Triisopropanolamine
Carbon dots/BiOBr
One-step hydrothermal synthesis
Exposed (001) facet

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of the BiOBr and CDs/BiOBr samples.
SEM images of (a) BiOBr; (b) CDs/BiOBr-1; (c) CDs/BiOBr-2; (d) CDs/BiOBr-3; (e) CDs/BiOBr-4 and (f) CDs/BiOBr-5.
TEM(a) and HRTEM(b) images of the CDs/BiOBr-2 sample.
XPS spectra of BiOBr, CDs/BiOBr-2 and CDs/BiOBr-5 samples: (a) C 1 s, (b) N 1 s, (c) O 1 s and (d) Bi 4f.
Effect of pH of precursor solution on CDs loading and morphology of composite catalyst.
(a) Photocatalytic degradation of MO under simulated sunlight; (b) UV–Vis DRS spectra of BiOBr and CDs/BiOBr samples.
The possible reaction process of CDs/BiOBr.

head.ce:title: One-step synthesis of CDs/BiOBr composite with high exposure of (001) facet: The effect of triisopropanolamine as the carbon source

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030024, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030024, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) modified BiOBr composites were synthesized by a facile one-step hydrothermal method using triisopropanolamine (TIPA). TIPA acts not only as the carbon source of CDs, but also as a structure modifier to obtain mesoporous BiOBr with dominant exposed (001) facets in the composite. Moreover, the amounts of CDs loaded are determined by the effect of TIPA on the pH of the reaction system rather than the addition amount itself. CDs/BiOBr exhibit enhanced adsorption and photodegradation activity of methyl orange compared with pure BiOBr. This work provides a facile approach in the structure controlling synthesis strategy of CDs-based nanohybrids.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), carbon nanoparticles with a diameter of less than 10 nm, have gained widespread attention thanks to their combination of superior properties [1–3]. Meanwhile, CDs-based nanohybrids, via the combination of CDs with various functional materials such as photocatalyst, electrocatalyst, biological materials, and so on, exhibit superior properties in their respective application fields with “a whole greater than the sum of the parts” effect [4–7]. The synthesis strategy is one of the most important considerations in the subject of CDs-based nanohybrids. Most strategies are to prepare carbon dots and nanomaterials separately, and then combine them together, which requires at least two steps [8–11]. The one-step synthesis method is to mix all the raw materials at one time and then process them to obtain the composite, which is a more facile and easy to operate strategy [12,13].

As a fascinating photocatalyst, BiOBr exhibits excellent performance in water splitting [14], CO2 reduction [15], nitrogen fixation [16], harmful pollutant decomposition [17–19], and so on. CDs have always been a star material in improving the performance of BiOBr by acting as a “conducting mediator” to enhance the charge conductivity and by increasing the light absorption range owing to its down- and up- conversion luminescence property [20–23].

The synthesis method of the CDs loaded BiOBr mainly includes two-step method and one-step method [24–27]. In the related literatures of one-step method, it was found that the carbon source has important effects on the structure and morphology of the obtained BiOBr. For example, Guan et al. [28] prepared CQDs/Bi/BiOBr samples by a one-pot solvothermal method using ethylene glycol as the carbon source. In the solvothermal process, CQDs could reduce part of BiO+ to metal Bi, and BiO+ ions and Br- ions combined with each other to form BiOBr nanosheets, which further self-assembled into BiOBr microspheres. CQDs and Bi could be controllably deposited on the surface of BiOBr microspheres in situ.

Triisopropanolamine (TIPA) is a carbon-containing compound with hydroxyl (–OH) and amino (-NR3) functional groups. In this work, for the first time, TIPA was used as a carbon source to synthesize CDs/BiOBr composites with exposed (001) facets in one-step by a hydrothermal treatment. In this regard, we mainly studied the effect of TIPA on the loading of CDs and the formation of BiOBr during the synthesis process. This work can provide a new method for the simple preparation of CDs/BiOBr composites.

Briefly speaking, 2 mmol of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O, 2 mmol of KBr and a certain amount of TIPA (nTIPA: nBiOBr = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5) were dissolved in 14 mL of ethanol to form a reaction precursor with stirring for 30 min, which was poured into the lining, followed by hydrothermal treatment at 120 °C for 16 h. After reaction, the product was centrifugally cleaned and dried. The samples obtained at the aforementioned amounts of TIPA added were named BiOBr, CDs/BiOBr-X (X = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5) separately.

The activity of the catalysts for photocatalytic degradation was evaluated by the following method. Firstly, 50 mg of catalyst was added to methyl orange (MO) solution (100 mL, 10 mg/L), and stirred to reach the catalyst adsorption equilibrium. Thereafter, with a Xe lamp (λ > 420 nm) with a light intensity of 120.2 klx, the mixture was irradiated. At certain time intervals, a suspension (4 mL) was extracted and the photocatalyst was removed by centrifugation. At last, the supernatant was tested by UV–Vis spectroscopy and the absorbance at 460 nm was recorded. The corresponding degradation efficiency (DE) of the catalyst can be calculated from Eq. (1):
(1)
DE
( % )
=
(
A 0
-
A t
)
/
A 0
× 100 % =
(
C 0
-
C t
)
/
C 0
× 100 %
Among them, A0 and C0 represent the initial absorbance and concentration of MO, and At and Ct represent the absorbance and concentration of the photocatalytic degradation in different time periods.

Fig. 1 shows the XRD patterns of BiOBr and CDs/BiOBr-X samples. The diffraction peaks of all samples correspond to the hexagonal phase BiOBr (PDF No.21-1272). But interestingly, after adding TIPA, the peaks representing (001) and (101) disappeared obviously and the peak representing (110) was more prominent in the spectrum, and the intensity of the (110) diffraction peak showed a trend of increasing first and then weakening with the increase of the amount of TIPA added. This phenomenon suggested that with the adding of TIPA, BiOBr tends to grow along the (110) crystal plane and expose the (001) plane perpendicular to (110) plane, and the (001) plane of BiOBr in the CDs/BiOBr-X2 sample is most exposed [29].The characteristic peaks of CDs were not found, which may be due to the small amounts of CDs and relatively uniform dispersion.

The morphology and color of samples are shown in Fig. 2 . Without TIPA assistant, the obtained BiOBr is composed of irregular lumps formed by agglomeration of nanoparticles. While with the addition of TIPA, the morphology of BiOBr is significantly influenced, flower-like microspheres by agglomeration of ultrathin nanosheets can be obtained. However, with the further increase of TIPA, the microspheres gradually collapsed and seemed to change back to the bulk structure of agglomeration of nanoparticles. The specific surface areas of pure BiOBr and CDs/BiOBr-2 were investigated by N2 adsorption–desorption method (Fig. S1a). According to the specific surface areas of BiOBr, CDs/BiOBr-2 and CDs/BiOBr-5, which were calculated by the BET method to be 19.02, 47.96 and 54.40 m2/g, respectively. According to the classification criteria of BDDT, both CDs/BiOBr-2 and CDs/BiOBr-5 can be classified as type IV curves, indicating the mesoporous structures [30]. In the relative partial pressure range of 0.45 to 1.0, the hysteresis loop of N-CDs/BiOBr-2 is H2 type, and the hysteresis loop of N-CDs/BiOBr-5 is H3 type, indicating that the pore size distribution of N-CDs/BiOBr-2 is wider. In addition, Fig. S1b pore size distribution map also proves this point. Meanwhile, it can be concluded that the CDs/BiOBr-5 has a more porous internal structure than the others.

The TEM and HRTEM of the complex were characterized using CDs/BiOBr-2 as a representative sample (Fig. 3 ). CDs/BiOBr-2 shows a sheet-like structure (Fig. 3a), corresponding to the petals in the SEM pattern. Fig. 3b shows its corresponding HRTEM image, in which two different lattice stripes can be seen. The lattice spacings of 0.221 nm and 0.277 nm correspond to the (101) facet of CDs and the (110) facet of hexagonal BiOBr, respectively, indicating that the CDs/BiOBr composite was successfully prepared in one-step using TIPA as the carbon source [31].

To further confirm the composition of obtained samples, the high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s, N 1s, Bi 4f and O 1s for BiOBr, CDs/BiOBr-2 and CDs/BiOBr-5 are shown in Fig. 4 . The spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 4a) confirmed the existence of CC/CN bond (peak at 284.8 eV), CO bond (peak at 288.2 eV), and CO bond (peak at 286.3 eV) [32,33], which commonly existed on the surface of CDs [31]. In N spectrum in Fig. 4b, the peak at 399.6 eV in two composites also confirms the presence of CN bond. Since TIPA contains nitrogen element, the CDs obtained by its carbonization are not unexpectedly nitrogen-doped CDs. N doping was reported to improve the photoluminescence quantum efficiency or result in excellent electronic and optical properties of CDs [32,34]. Therefore, it is possible to endow BiOBr with excellent properties. In the N1s spectrum of CDs/BiOBr-5, there is an extra-peak at 401.8 eV, which should belong to the peak of N (C)3 bond originated from TIPA [24]. This result indicated that more uncarbonized TIPA was adsorbed on the surface of CDs/BiOBr-5 than that of CDs/BiOBr-2.

Fig. 4c showed the high-resolution spectra of O 1s. The three fitted peaks for BiOBr were at 528.9, 530.5, and 531.9 eV, corresponding to surface adsorbed oxygen, CO [35,36], and surface hydroxyl groups [37], respectively. Bi 4f spectrum of samples in Fig. 4d, indicating Bi exists in the Bi3+ form, but the binding energy is slightly shifted due to the influence of surface-bonded materials, which also proves the tight connection between CDs and BiOBr.

The above results strongly prove that the CDs are successfully loaded on the surface of BiOBr nanosheets. The loading of CDs also caused the CDs/BiOBr composite to exhibit a brownish-yellow color (insets of Fig. 2). Interestingly, the corresponding sample color showed a trend of darkening and then lightening with the increase of TIPA, indicating the loading of CDs is not as high as the addition amount of TIPA. And the content of C in BiOBr obtained by XPS well confirms this point, which is 44.22%, 57.08% and 46.94% for BiOBr, CDs/BiOBr-2 and CDs/BiOBr-5, respectively.

Therefore, in order to find out the reason, we tested the pH of precursor solutions of BiOBr, CDs/BiOBr-1, CDs/BiOBr-2, CDs/BiOBr-3, CDs/BiOBr-4 and CDs/BiOBr-5, and it was 0.28, 0.38, 0.66, 1.22, 4.69 and 5.66, respectively. That is reasonable that TIPA is a weak alkaline substance, and following the increase of TIPA, the pH value will gradually increase. It has been reported that pH is a serious factor during synthesis of CDs [38]. Therefore, when the pH reaches a certain amount, it may be unfavorable to the carbonization of TIPA, resulting in a lower CDs loading. To verify this conjecture, we designed a set of comparative experiments and the results are shown in Fig. 5 . For the CDs/BiOBr-2 composite material, after the pH value of precursor solutions was increased to 5.66 with 5 mol/L NaOH solution, a sample with lighter brown color was obtained (named CDs/BiOBr-2*), and SEM image indicated that its microsphere structure had collapsed. For the CDs/BiOBr-5 composite material, after the pH value of precursor solutions was reduced to 0.66 with 5 mol/L HCl solution, a sample with much darker brown color was obtained (named CDs/BiOBr-5*), and the morphology also changed from random agglomerates to microspheres composed of nanosheets. Besides that, compared the sample CDs/BiOBr-2 to CDs/BiOBr-5*, under the same pH, higher TIPA adding can lead to higher CDs loadings according to the darker color in CDs/BiOBr-5*. The results showed that pH played a decisive role in the loading of CDs and also affected the structure of the as-prepared BiOBr. If the pH of precursor solutions exceeds 0.66, the process of converting TIPA to CDs loaded on BiOBr would be inhibited. Extended from this, tunable loading of CDs based composites can be achieved by controlling the pH and TIPA addition.

From the above results, a possible reaction process can be obtained as shown in Scheme 1 . The hydroxyl (OH) of TIPA can interact with the Bi atom of (001) facets and occupy these facets, which will reduce the growth rate followed [001] direction, eventually leading to the formation of nanosheets structure of BiOBr with exposed (001) facets [39]. The TIPA on the surface of BiOBr can be finally carbonized into CDs at a relative acidic condition. The nanosheets decorated with CDs then self-assembles to form the microspheres. As the pH increases to exceed 0.66, the carbonization of TIPA weakens, and TIPA acts more like a kind of organic sacrificial template to develop porous BiOBr [40].

The properties of synthesized samples were simply evaluated by photocatalytic degradation of MO under simulated solar light irradiation. As shown in Fig. 6 a, before lighting on, the MO adsorption ability of samples increased following the increase of TIPA, which should be due to the increase of the specific surface area of BiOBr with the addition of TIPA. Coupled with the effect of CDs loading in improving the photocatalytic activity, which may improve the absorption of light as shown in UV–vis DRS of samples in Fig. 6b and/or celebrate the transformation of photogenerated charge of BiOBr [41,42], all composites exhibited better photocatalytic degradation of MO than pure BiOBr.

In conclusion, we have developed a one-step method for the synthesis of mesoporous CDs/BiOBr composites with high exposure (001) facets by using TIPA as the carbon source. The double effect of TIPA on the loading amounts of CDs and structure of BiOBr are studied by XRD, SEM, HRTEM, BET, XPS and DRS analysis, and a possible formation mechanism is proposed. Owing to the increased specific surface area and CDs loading, the synthesized composites show enhanced properties on adsorption and photodegradation of MO. This work provides a new idea for the preparation of CDs/BiOBr composites and offers a method to control synthesis of mesoporous BiOBr with exposed (001) facets.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (nos. 21808151 and 21978187), Natural Science Foundation for Young Scientists of Shanxi Province (no. 201901D211058) and Science Technology Innovation Project of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi (no. 2019L0143).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2022.109790.
